summary:
The Baroque Period in Music (c. 1600–1750 AD) was an era of extraordinary musical development in Europe during which composers created new forms (opera, oratorio, concerto, sonata, fugue) that became the foundations of the Western classical tradition, producing the works of Monteverdi, Vivaldi, Handel, [[event:3260|Bach]], Purcell, Corelli, and Telemann — a generation of composers whose music remains central to the concert repertoire. The Baroque musical aesthetic emphasized emotional expression, dramatic contrast, elaborate ornamentation, and the basso continuo (a bass line with harmony improvised by a harpsichord or organ, providing the harmonic foundation of ensemble music) — characteristics that paralleled the visual Baroque's emphasis on movement, drama, and emotional intensity.

The period's innovations were transformative. Opera, invented around 1600 by the Florentine Camerata group seeking to recreate ancient Greek drama, rapidly evolved from court entertainment to public spectacle; Monteverdi's Orfeo (1607) established it as a serious art form. The concerto grosso (developed by Corelli and perfected by Vivaldi) contrasted a small soloist group with a full orchestra, creating dramatic dialogue. George Frideric Handel's oratorios — Messiah (1741) most famously — created a new genre of unstaged dramatic sacred music accessible to middle-class audiences at public concerts. Johann Sebastian [[event:3260|Bach]] synthesized all Baroque forms in works of mathematical complexity and spiritual depth that remained underappreciated in his lifetime but were rediscovered by Mendelssohn in the 19th century and are now considered the summit of the tradition. The Baroque period ended around 1750 (marked by Bach's death) as the simpler, more 'natural' Classical style of Haydn and the young [[event:3261|Mozart]] emerged in reaction to Baroque complexity.
